explanation.text: Trent is the younger brother, yet his new red height mark is above Troy's for the first time. Troy's disbelief and Trent's delight show that this unexpected change takes both brothers by surprise.

At first Trent wants to work on his aquarium and asks to postpone being measured. Once Mother says his mark is higher than Troy's, he squeals, checks the marks, and celebrates, showing that the news creates his interest.
Troy volunteers to stand back-to-back with Trent because he wants to settle the height question as soon as possible. Thus, “lay this matter to rest” means find out the truth and end the debate.

The red mark is higher than the blue mark, so choice B is the strongest evidence offered for the claim: it directly compares the boys' recorded heights. The mark reflects an earlier measurement rather than conclusively proving their current heights, but the other choices do not compare their heights at all.
In the mirror, Troy sees the height difference himself and calls the reflected evidence undeniable. That confirmation settles the conflict by proving that Trent really is taller.
The discovery that Trent's door mark is higher than Troy's begins the brothers' argument and leads to every later comparison. Because it captures the story's central event, it belongs in a summary.

Rupa tells Dadima that her saris are beautiful but immediately asks whether wearing them every day becomes tiring. This question suggests she likes their appearance but would not want to wear one daily herself.
Dadima demonstrates that a sari can serve as a fan, a pouch for shells, an umbrella, and even a hiding place. These varied practical uses support her claim that she can always do something with it.

Dadima explains that one sari was her first, one was worn when she traveled from India to America, and one was her wedding sari. Showing them lets her share memories of important moments with her granddaughters.
The repeated word “unfold” shows that the girls must keep opening more and more material before reaching the end. That continued action emphasizes how large and long the sari is.

Dadima's three special saris are connected to important occasions: receiving her first sari, moving from India to America, and getting married. Those examples directly link clothing customs with meaningful life events.

The baby-sitter realizes that calming Billy will require a new kind of expertise, then uses the sports trophies as inspiration for the “funderstorm” game. Linking Billy's interests to the storm shows a good idea taking shape.

In paragraph 6, Billy reveals the problem that thunderstorms frighten him. By paragraph 16, the baby-sitter has invented “funderstorm,” a game that distracts and calms him, so the later paragraph provides the solution.
The thunder rattles the windows, and Billy immediately starts to dive back under the bed. His attempt to hide shows that the sound “rattles his nerves” by making him feel frightened.

By the end, Billy says he cannot wait for the next storm and gives the baby-sitter a knowing look; the narrator therefore expects another call whenever storm clouds appear. These details show how much Billy likes the games.
The narrator uses “I” while describing how the Clarksons hired the sitter, what the sitter thinks, and how the games are invented. Therefore, the baby-sitter is the character telling the story.

Billy begins under the bed, frightened by thunder, but timed games turn each flash and boom into a challenge until his worried look disappears. This change shows that focusing on other activities can make a scary event easier.
